coredata.dc:title: A cellulose nanocrystal-carbon dots@cholestrol fluorescent probe for imaging of plasma membrane with extended time scale
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuqian
Liang, Fangyuan
Sun, Janglei
Xu, Xuan
Deng, Chao
Sun, Ran
Liu, Chao
Xiao, Huining
coredata.dc:description: Despite the advancement of plasma membrane imaging techniques, there is an unmet need for fluorescent probes which can “light up” the lipid bilayer with an extended time scale. In this work, a novel fluorescent probe is developed for imaging of plasma membrane. The probe comprises a cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) scaffold derived from low-cost wood pulp, carbon dots (CDs) fluorophore that is in-situ synthesized on the surface of CNC, and cholesterol (Chol) ligand with aminated polyethylene glycol (PEG) spacer. In the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol configuration, the Chol ligand provides excellent binding affinity onto plasma membrane due to its hydrophobic nature, while CDs contribute to the marvelous optical stability, as well as the high quantum yield of the fluorescent probe. For CNC scaffold, their unique morphological property allows the assembling of dense Chol ligands, and more importantly, impedes the diffusions of imaging moieties into the cytoplasm. Therefore, the fluorescent probe is featured with remarkable specificity and satisfying plasma membrane imaging time window, which covers the co-incubation time from 30 to 90 min. In contrast, the mono-dispersed CDs@PEG-Chol probe can be facilely internalized by the cells at a co-incubation time as short as 20 min. CNC is proven to be responsible for the different imaging time scales by providing the confinement effects. The proposed fluorescent probe is believed to be a promising tool for studying the plasma membrane behaviors associated with cellular physiological processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Cellulose nanocrystal
Cholesterol
Fluorescent probe
Plasma membrane imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and imaging of the plasma membrane
Molecular dynamic (MD) simulations of the ligand diffusions with the lipid bilayer
Facile preparation of CNC-CDs via hydrolysis-carbonization tactic
Dense NH2-PEG-Chol ligand grafting on CNC-CDs
Superior optical and colloidal stability of the fluorescent probe
Excellent biocompatibility of the fluorescent probe
Plasma membrane imaging with extended time window
Validation of the confinement effect of CNCs in the fluorescent probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of (a) CNCs and (b) CNC-CDs. (B) Zeta potentials and the carboxyl contents of CNCs and CNC-CDs. (C) X-ray diffractograms of CNCs and CNC-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) CNC-CDs and (B) free CDs under varying excitations. The inset photographs show the CNC-CDs dispersion under the ambient light and UV light (365 nm), respectively. (C) Absorption spectra of CNCs, CNC-CDs and free CDs. (D) Fluorescence decay curves of CNC-CDs and free CDs.

(A) AFM images (top panel) and the corresponding height of the CNC-CDs and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol with varying ligand density (bottom panel). (B) Hydrodynamic size of the CNC-CDs and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol. (C) High-resolution C 1 s signal on XPS spectra of CNC-CDs and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol. The inset figures show the corresponding N 1 s signal. (D) FT-IR spectra of CNC-CDs, CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol, and the NH2-PEG-Chol ligand.

(A) Fluorescence intensities of CNC-CDs and fluorescent probe upon the continuous excitation for 12 h. The light source was a xenon arc lamp (365 nm), power of which was fixed at 150 W. (B) Hydrodynamic size and (C) AFM images, as well as the measured height (bottom curves) of a) CNC-CDs and b) fluorescent probe in the presence of cell culture media.
Viability of the MCF-7 cells in the presence of different concentrations of probe upon being treated for 8, 16, and 24 h.

Fluorescent and fluorescent/bright field overlaid images of MCF-7 cells co-incubated with the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol probe and the CD@PEG-Chol probe, respectively, for varying time. Scale bar: 20 µm. The histograms below each image represent the fluorescence intensities calculated by ImageJ software for the cropped areas (200 × 200 pixels) at cell boundaries (red squares, corresponding to red histograms) and cytoplasm (blue squares, corresponding to blue histograms).
Fluorescent and fluorescent/bright field overlaid images of HeLa cells and HEK 293 cells treated with the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol probe as a function of time. Scale bar: 20 µm.

(A) Configuration images illustrate the dissolving processes of Chol and NH2-PEG-Chol ligands in the plasma membrane model. The simulation time is 0–40 ns. (B) Evolutions of the centroids distances between the ligands and the lipid bilayer. (C) Changes in the contact areas of the ligands and the lipid bilayer as a function of simulation time.

Illustration of the construction procedure of CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol probe and its application in plasma membrane imaging. The probe is constructed by conjugating NH2-PEG-Chol ligand onto CNC-CDs nanohybrid, which is stepwise prepared from wood pulp following an acid-hydrolysis and in-situ CDs synthesis procedure.

head.ce:title: A cellulose nanocrystal-carbon dots@cholestrol fluorescent probe for imaging of plasma membrane with extended time scale

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources and Joint International Research Lab of Lignocellulosic Functional Materials, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources and Joint International Research Lab of Lignocellulosic Functional Materials, Nanjing Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources and Joint International Research Lab of Lignocellulosic Functional Materials, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick E3B5A3, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick E3B5A3, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite the advancement of plasma membrane imaging techniques, there is an unmet need for fluorescent probes which can “light up” the lipid bilayer with an extended time scale. In this work, a novel fluorescent probe is developed for imaging of plasma membrane. The probe comprises a cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) scaffold derived from low-cost wood pulp, carbon dots (CDs) fluorophore that is in-situ synthesized on the surface of CNC, and cholesterol (Chol) ligand with aminated polyethylene glycol (PEG) spacer. In the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol configuration, the Chol ligand provides excellent binding affinity onto plasma membrane due to its hydrophobic nature, while CDs contribute to the marvelous optical stability, as well as the high quantum yield of the fluorescent probe. For CNC scaffold, their unique morphological property allows the assembling of dense Chol ligands, and more importantly, impedes the diffusions of imaging moieties into the cytoplasm. Therefore, the fluorescent probe is featured with remarkable specificity and satisfying plasma membrane imaging time window, which covers the co-incubation time from 30 to 90 min. In contrast, the mono-dispersed CDs@PEG-Chol probe can be facilely internalized by the cells at a co-incubation time as short as 20 min. CNC is proven to be responsible for the different imaging time scales by providing the confinement effects. The proposed fluorescent probe is believed to be a promising tool for studying the plasma membrane behaviors associated with cellular physiological processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The plasma membrane plays the role of more than a simple barrier that isolates the cytoplasm from ambiance [1]. Biologists have proved that the plasma membrane is capable of dominating a variety of cellular events, such as substance transportation, signal transduction, pathogen recognition, and so forth [2–4] . Therefore, exploring imaging techniques that can benefit the studies in understanding the molecular basis and the relevant biological activities of plasma membranes has been one of the most cutting-edge topics in this area [5]. Fluorescence-based imaging technique possesses the most widespread acceptance due to its intrinsic high spatiotemporal resolution, and more importantly, the facility in devising fluorescent probes capable of “lighting up” the micro-objects to be investigated [6]. By elegantly engineering the fluorescent probes, overwhelmingly with the architecture of fluorophore@spacer-ligand, imaging of a plethora of plasma membrane components (proteins, raft domains, lipid bilayer, etc.), or microenvironments (acidity, viscosity, tension, etc.) has been realized [7–12]. However, suffering from their photo-bleaching readiness under laser irradiation or internalization easiness by living cells, the probes, in most instances, can provide narrow imaging time windows, which has largely limited their applications in tracing membrane dynamics. The unmet need for plasma membrane imaging over extended time scales has prompted researchers to develop novel fluorescent probes via, on one hand, boosting the optical stability of fluorophore [13], and on the other hand, intensifying their binding affinity to plasma membrane [14].

As ideal alternatives to organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have attracted tremendous attention in bio-imaging due to their nontrivial optical properties including narrow and tunable emission profile, large stocks shift, and superior anti-photobleaching capacity [15]. Since the first attempt to construct a QDs-based plasma membrane probe with long-term stability, they have been extensively investigated in displaying the structural, signal, or environmental changes of cell surfaces [16–19]. In comparison to QDs, those containing cadmium in particular, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging carbon nanomaterials featured with not only optical stability, but also cost-effectiveness, and profound biocompatibility [20]. With the purpose of enhancing the binding affinity of probes with plasma membrane, a variety of ligands, such as cholesterol, fullerene, dodecane, etc., were conjugated on the probes [21–23]. Hydrophobicity of the ligands enabled the probes to be localized at the cell surface. However, mono-dispersed fluorescent probes are always suffered from the facility of being internalized by living cells [24,25]. Generally, membrane imaging can be achieved at time scales as short as several minutes. One possible strategy to address this problem is to fabricate polymeric fluorescent probes which are densely modified by the lipid bilayer anchoring ligands and fluorophores [26]. The greatly increased ligand amount on each probe provided avid binding of the probes and plasma membrane. More importantly, the polymer backbones, such as polyfluorene, poly(vinyl alcohol), or chitosan derivative [27–29], inhibited the diffusions of fluorescent moieties into the cytoplasm. What complicated the explorations and applications of the polymeric fluorescent probes is the multi-step synthesis procedure, which was laborious-consuming and cost-ineffective.

Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are produced by the “top-down” degradations of sources like wood, cotton, ramie, etc [30]. As a “rising star” nanomaterial, CNCs have attracted much attention due to their superior properties over petroleum-based materials, including environmental friendliness, excellent biocompatibility, and preparation and modification easiness. Via fluorescent functionalization, the emissive CNCs have found their ample application scopes in biomedical areas [31]. A plethora of sensory systems that are responsive to metal ions, organic compounds, and physical stimuli [32–34], or fluorescent probes for “lighting up” the cytoplasm have been devised using CNCs as the scaffold [35,36]. Encouraged by their rod-shaped morphology with a high aspect ratio and large surface area with abundant reactive sites, we challenge to construct a fluorescent CNC-based probe for imaging of plasma membrane with high resolution, as well as extended retention time. As shown in Scheme 1, bleached wood pulp is hydrolyzed by citric acid (CA) to extract CNCs. Esterification between the carboxyl group and the C6 hydroxyl group of cellulose leads to the dense modification of CNCs by CA. Benefited from the carbonization facility of CA, synthesis of CDs on the surface of CNCs is achievable, rendering the CNCs with bright fluorescence. The CNC-CDs complex is rich in carboxyl groups, allowing the construction of fluorescent probes via efficient grafting of aminated polyethylene glycol-cholesterol (NH2-PEG-Chol). Being featured with prominent photo-bleaching resistance, in combination with the significant ligand amount, the proposed fluorescent probe is capable of labeling the plasma membrane with a satisfying time scale.

Bleached wood pulp was commercially obtained from Aracruz Cellulose, (Brazil). CA (99.5%) was supplied by J & K Chemical (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)−3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC, 98%) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NH2-PEG-Chol and NH2-PEG-rhodamine B (NH2-PEG-RhoB), in which the molecular weight of PEG was 5000 Da, were received from Xinqiao Biotech. (Hangzhou, China). Glutathione (GSH), histidine (His), glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum, and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from Fcmacs Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Pure water was obtained with a Pall Cascade AN system.

The fluorescent CNCs were prepared by hydrolyzing wood pulp with CA and further in-situ synthesizing CDs on the surface of CNCs. Typically, 20 g of CA was added into 5 mL of pure water and heated to 100 ℃ until the CA was dissolved. Afterward, 0.5 g of wood pulp was added into the CA solution and hydrolyzed at 110 ℃ for 8 h under continuous magnetic stirring. The hydrolysis procedure was terminated by adding 200 mL of pure water to the mixture. Subsequently, pH of the mixture was adjusted to 6.0 with 3 M of NaOH. Microwave treatment of the solution was conducted to synthesize CDs on the surface of as-prepared CNCs. In detail, 60 mL of the hydrolysis product and 2 g of urea were added into a 150 mL flask, which was placed in a microwave reactor system, and was heated to 180 ℃ for 5 min at a power of 600 W. The carbonization product was filtrated through a 0.22 µm filter membrane till a neutral and non-fluorescent filtrate was obtained. The CNC-CDs were mono-dispersed in 100 mL of pure water by sonication at a frequency of 20 kHz for 2 h with 2 s intervals and was further purified by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) to remove the large aggregates. The CNC-CDs was stored at 4 ℃ in PBS buffer for characterizations or further use. For comparing the optical properties, free CDs were synthesized using 5 g of CA and 2 g of urea under the consistent carbonization conditions for CNC-CDs synthesis. The CDs were dialyzed (MWCO = 500 Da) for 24 h before use.

The fluorescent probe with the structure of CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol was constructed via the carbodiimide reaction [37]. The CNC-CDs dispersion was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the CNC-CDs were re-dispersed in 4-morpholine ethane sulfonic acid (MES) buffer (100 mM, pH 6.0) with a concentration of 1 mg/mL. Under vigorous stirring, 1 mL of EDC solution (100 mg/mL in MES buffer) was added into 10 mL of the CNC-CDs dispersion for the activation of carboxyl groups. After 30 min of activation, varying amounts (0–12 mg) of NH2-PEG-Chol powder were added into the dispersion, which was further stirred for 4 h. For removing the unbounded NH2-PEG-Chol, as well as the excess EDC, the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol nanocomposites were separated from the dispersion via centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 10 min and washed 3 times with pure water. The probe was stored in PBS buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4) for further use. For evaluating the conjugation density of NH2-PEG-Chol, a fluorescently-labeled NH2-PEG chain (NH2-PEG-RhoB) was conjugated on the surface of CNC-CDs under the identical conditions with those for NH2-PEG-Chol coupling. The fluorescence intensities of RhoB (EX: 550 nm; EM: 582 nm) in solutions before and after the coupling experiment was used to calculate the conjugation density.

With the purpose of clarifying the role CNCs played in the fluorescent probe, the mono-dispersed CDs@PEG-Chol probe was also constructed and applied for plasma membrane imaging. To a CDs solution (1 mL containing 0.2 mg of CDs), 2 mg of NH2-PEG-Chol and 0.1 mL of EDC solution (100 mg/mL) were added. The mixture was continuously stirred for 4 h, followed by the addition of 5 mL of isopropanol. After the removal of soluble free CDs, the precipitated CDs@PEG-Chol was washed 2 times with isopropanol. Finally, the CDs@PEG-Chol probe was dispersed in 1 mL of PBS buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and imaging of the plasma membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Both cancer cells (MCF-7 and HeLa) and normal human embryonic kidney cells (HEK 293) were used as the model imaging targets in this work. The cells were respectively cultured in DMEM containing 10% FBS and 1% penicillin streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified environment containing 5% CO2. The cells were cultured until 60% confluence was reached before addition of the fluorescent probe.

The cytotoxicity of the probe was assessed by the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)−2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide) assay. The native MCF-7 cells were treated with varying concentrations of the fluorescent probe for 8, 16, and 24 h and subsequently stained with MTT for 4 h. After carefully washing the cells with PBS buffer 3 times, 150 μL of DMSO was added to each culture well. The absorption value at 490 nm was measured after 10 min of shaking.

For imaging the plasma membrane bilayer, the cells were cultured on the glass coverslips (diameter: 18 mm), which were placed in the culture plate wells. 200 μL of the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol dispersion (2 mg/mL in PBS buffer) was added into the culture media (1.8 mL). After incubation for varying times, the cells were washed three times with PBS buffer and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde fix solution for 15 min. After two times of washing with PBS buffer, the cells were finally imaged by a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a slit width of 1.5 nm. The transient fluorescence measurements were performed on an FLS980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrophotometers (Edinburgh instruments). The scanning step was fixed at 0.2 ns, and the excitation wavelength was fixed at 410 nm. The samples were placed in a quartz cuvette for fluorescence measurements. The Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements of CNC-CDs and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol were performed on a Zetasizer Nano-ZS particle instrument (Malvern, UK). The samples were dispersed in PBS buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4) for the measurements. Morphologies of CNC-CDs and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-1400 instrument (JEOL, Japan) and MFP-30 Bio atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, UK). The samples were dispersed in pure water with a concentration of 0.05 wt% and dropped on Cu grids or mica plates for TEM and AFM measurements, respectively. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of CNCs and CNC-CDs were acquired with an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) at a wide-angle (5–50°) scan at the rate of 10°/min and the crystallinity index ( I
C) was determined according to the equation I
C = ( I
200 - I
am)/ I
200 × 100%, where I
200 was the maximum intensity at 2θ = 22.5° corresponded to both crystalline and amorphous regions. I
am referred to the intensity at 2θ = 18°, which was linked to the amorphous region. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) characterization was recorded on a Vertex 80 V FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, German) by the use of KBr pellets containing samples. The chemical composition of all samples was analyzed on an AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells were gained from an LSM710 laser confocal microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

To ensure data reliability, the characterizations were performed based on five independent measurements for the calculations of average values and errors. The Student-t test was employed for evaluating the significance levels. In the plasma membrane imaging experiments, the images were analyzed by the use of ImageJ software (V1.8.0). In detail, five randomly selected areas (200 × 200 pixels) at the boundaries, and five randomly selected areas (200 × 200 pixels) in the inner parts of cells were cropped and the green-channel brightness in the squares were recorded. Afterwards, the statistical tests were performed to define the plasma membrane imaging results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamic (MD) simulations of the ligand diffusions with the lipid bilayer
ce:section.ce:para: For comparing the differences between NH2-PEG-Chol and Chol in the interactions with cell membrane. MD simulations of the diffusion processes of the ligands into the lipid bilayer were performed by the use of the GROMACS package, version 5.0.5 [38]. The chemical, as well as the topological structures of the NH2-PEG-Chol and Chol, were constructed first. The number of (CH2CH2O) units was 113 for the PEG with a molecular weight of 5000 Da. The membrane model was constructed with 288 DPPC (1,2-dipalmitoyl-DL-alpha-phosphatidylcholine) molecules in a simulation box of 12 nm. The structural and topological file of DPPC was downloaded from the LipidBook [39]. The NH2-PEG-Chol or Chol was placed outside of the bilayer. After the operations of solvent (water) filling, charge equilibrium, and energy minimization, a 40 ns of MD analysis was performed to study the interactions of NH2-PEG-Chol or Chol with the lipid bilayer. The molecular graphics were produced with the VMD program [40]. The centroids distances, as well as the contact areas, of NH2-PEG-Chol/lipid bilayer or Chol/lipid bilayer were calculated with MDAnalysis Python Package [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facile preparation of CNC-CDs via hydrolysis-carbonization tactic
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the efficient CNC extraction protocol by the use of organic acids, as well as the extensive adoption of organic acids in the synthesis of CDs, we designed a novel hydrolysis-carbonization tactic for the preparation of CNC-CDs from the raw material (bleached wood pulp). In the hydrolysis-carbonization procedure, tricarboxylic CA plays the essential roles as 1) the acidolysis agent to remove the amorphous parts of cellulose, 2) the stabilizer modified on CNCs via the Fisher-Speier esterification, and 3) the carbon source to form CDs on the surface of CNCs. The successively produced CNCs and CNC-CDs are characterized in detail. As shown in Fig. 1A, the TEM images of CNCs and CNC-CDs reveal their rod-shaped structure and the uniform spreading of CDs on the surface of CNCs experienced carbonization. Both CNCs and CNC-CDs possess excellent mono-dispersity, which is benefited from the electrostatic repulsion among the crystals. For CNCs, the Zeta potential and carboxyl group content are −48.7 ± 2.1 mV and 0.87 ± 0.04 mmol/g (Fig. 1B), respectively. The content of carboxyl group is comparable to that of CNCs prepared by the typical TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-piperidinyloxy) oxidation method [42], implying an extremely high CA modification density. As a result of CA dehydration upon the carefully controlled carbonization treatment, the decrement in charge density is observed for CNC-CDs. However, negligible impact on the dispersity is produced because of the mild loss of the carboxyl group. Moreover, the crystallinity of CNCs can be well maintained during the entire preparation process. As shown in Fig. 1C, signals at 14.9°, 16.5°, 22.7°, and 34.5° are attributed to the (1ī0), (110), (200), and (004) facets of cellulose, respectively. The crystallinity indexes are calculated to be 88.4 ± 0.9% for CNC-CDs, extremely close to those of CNCs (89.7 ± 0.6%). The high crystallinity of CNC-CDs indicates that CNCs are not participated in the formation of CDs except for providing a substrate. These results suggest an ideal nano-platform for the construction of fluorescent probe with desirable mono-dispersity and structural stability is obtained.

Another essential point to be concerned about in terms of their imaging application for the CNC-CDs is the optical property. For elaborating the differences between CNC-CDs and free CDs synthesized in the absence of CNCs, the optical character of free CDs was parallelly investigated. As shown in Fig. 2A, the CNC-CDs reveals an emission peak consistently centered at 500 nm with the excitation wavelength ranging from 340 nm to 420 nm, and shows the maximum at the excitation of 410 nm. The emission character indicates that the CNC-CDs is extremely suitable for being observed under the confocal microscope equipped with the 405 nm laser source. The bright green fluorescence is also noticeable under the irradiation of a UV lamp (365 nm) (inset photograph of Fig. 2A). For the free CDs, however, an evident excitation-dependent emission character can be observed (Fig. 2B). From the absorption spectra, it is found that the peaks at 260 and 416 nm appear for CNC-CDs, which can be attributed to the π-π* and n-π transitions of in-situ synthesized CDs. In the spectrum of free CDs, an additional peak at 335 nm is observed (Fig. 2C), which corresponds to its complex excitation character (Fig. S1A). In combination with the high-resolution TEM image and the size distribution (3.91 ± 1.56 nm) of free CDs, (Fig. S1B and C), we deduce that impurities with conjugated structures exist in the free CDs solution, and uniformity of the CDs is poor, although dialysis was performed. Benefited from the separation easiness provided by CNC substrates, the CNC-CDs complex contains no impurities except for CDs with satisfying uniformity. As such, the emission of CNC-CDs exhibits unapparent excitation-dependence. The shortened fluorescence lifetime of free CDs (Fig. 2D) can confirm our conjecture since the organic or polymeric fluorophores possess shorter fluorescence lifetimes than the crystalline emitters. Using Rho6G as the standard, the quantum yield of CNC-CDs is measured as 27.1 ± 3.3%, which is close to the free CDs (32.4 ± 2.6%). These results confirm that the hydrolysis-carbonization is a facile and cost-effective tactic for the fabrication of fluorescent CNCs, which is a promising scaffold for “lighting up” certain subcellular structures after reasonable modification with targeting ligands.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dense NH2-PEG-Chol ligand grafting on CNC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent probe was constructed by functionalization of the emissive CNC-CDs with NH2-PEG-Chol, in which the Chol is the ligand capable of inserting the lipid bilayer, PEG is significant in the elimination of steric hindrance, and the terminal amino group is pivotal for the robust conjugation of the units by virtue of the covalent reaction. The conjugation is mediated by EDC, which can activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNC-CDs. With the purpose of finding the optimal grafting density of the ligand, the mass ratio of NH2-PEG-Chol to CNC-CDs was adjusted. As shown in Fig. 3A, obviously, height of the nanocomposites increases gradually along with the improved grafting amount of NH2-PEG-Chol. At an NH2-PEG-Chol to CNC-CDs mass ratio of 0.9:1, the height of CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol reaches 21.2 nm, which is 67% larger than that of CNC-CDs. Surprisingly, further increment of the ligand amount induces the aggregation of the nanocomposites. We speculate that its mono-dispersity is destroyed due to the excessive modification by the electroneutral Chol. The Zeta potential measurement of the nanocomposites demonstrates the negative correlation between the surface charge density and grafting density of NH2-PEG-Chol (Fig. S2). The DLS measurements exhibit a consistent trend in the hydrodynamic size evolution to the morphological changes (Fig. 3B). For the nanocomposites constructed by a mass ratio of 0.9:1, the average hydrodynamic size increases by 114 nm to that of bare CNC-CDs. However, unreasonable size growth for the nanocomposites with excessive NH2-PEG-Chol (1.2:1) can be observed because of the aggregates. With regard to a fluorescent probe for plasma membrane imaging, the NH2-PEG-Chol to CNC-CDs mass ratio of 0.9:1 is adopted based on the following considerations: 1) a maximum Chol density is achieved on the premise of unaffected mono-dispersity of the probe, and 2) electrostatic repulsion between the fluorescent probe (−18.6 ± 3.2 mV) and negatively-charged cell surface can be as possibly avoided.

For revealing the exact conjugation amount of NH2-PEG-Chol, an emissive NH2-PEG-RhoB was modified on the surface of CNC-CDs under identical conditions with NH2-PEG-Chol grafting. Relying on the canonical plotting between the NH2-PEG-RhoB concentration and the fluorescence intensity, its molar change is quantifiable by recording the fluorescence of RhoB before and after the conjugation procedure (Fig. S3). It is estimated that 0.13 μmol of the ligand is grafted on 1 mg of CNC-CDs scaffold. The excellent grafting efficiency is benefited from the large carboxyl group content of CNC-CDs and is considered to provide the high binding affinity of the fluorescent probe onto the plasma membrane.

The chemical properties of CNC-CDs and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol nanocomposite were characterized in detail for providing insight view of the emissive scaffold and fluorescent probe preparations. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fitted C 1 s signal on the XPS spectrum of CNC-CDs emphasizes the C-C, C-OH, C-O-C, and CO bonding by showing the peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.5 eV, 288 eV, and 288.9 eV, respectively [43]. The appearance of CO bond indicates the introduction of CA onto the CNC substrate in the hydrolysis process, regardless of the subsequent carbonization. Upon grafting of the NH2-PEG-Chol ligand, signal of C-N bond at 287.4 eV in C 1 s spectrum, as well as the signal of N 1 s at 396–408 eV, demonstrates the formation of amido linkage between the CNC-CDs scaffold and the ligand [44]. Except for the C-N linkage, the characteristic vibrations of PEG chain and Chol head can be observed on the FT-IR spectrum of CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 3D, the peaks at 1241 cm−1, 944 cm−1, and 844 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C-O-C in PEG, bending vibration of C-H in Chol (cyclopentane polyhydrophenanthrene nuclei), and rocking vibration of -CH2- in Chol (hydrophobic derivative), respectively. The peaks are shown in both the spectra of NH2-PEG-Chol and CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol nanocomposite, however, are absent in the spectrum of CNC-CDs. Therefore, the FT-IR result verifies the successful construction of the fluorescent probe as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Superior optical and colloidal stability of the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Having validated the robust structure of the fluorescent probe, its photo-bleaching resistance and dispersion stability in the complex biological environment was further investigated for the demand of plasma membrane imaging. The photo-bleaching resistance test was performed by exposing the fluorescent probe to irradiation for 12 h and recording the fluorescent intensity at intervals of 20 min. As shown in Fig. 4A, both the fluorescent probe and CNC-CDs exhibit fluorescent intensities greater than 85% and 75% upon excitations for 6 h and 12 h, respectively, suggesting the sufficient optical stability of the CDs in the probe and allowing the long-term “lighting up” of the object to be monitored. Moreover, the fluorescence is tolerant towards a wide pH range (5.0–9.0) and high salt stress (3 M) (Fig. S4). Reliability, as an essential demand for bio-sensing events, should also be valued for bio-imaging studies. The biomolecules which can accumulate the fluorescent probe are supposed to give rise to the false-positive bio-imaging results. Therefore, the interaction between the probe and the biomolecules was investigated by measuring the dispersity of the probe in the cell culture media. For clarifying the role of the ligand in improving the reliability of plasma membrane imaging, the interaction between the CNC-CDs and the biological components was investigated in parallel. Noticeably, the hydrodynamic diameter of fluorescent probe in the culture media (304 ± 38.3) is close to that in the buffer (273 ± 20.7 nm). In contrast, the size of CNC-CDs significantly increases as they are transferred into the culture media, implying the occurrence of aggregation (Fig. 4B). The dispersion states of the fluorescent probe and CNC-CDs are intuitionally displayed in the AFM images (Fig. 4C). In comparison to the mono-dispersed fluorescent probe, the stacked CNC-CDs reveal greatly improved height. The outperforming colloidal stability of the probe is benefitted from the grafting of NH2-PEG-Chol ligand, which is capable of efficiently shielding the adsorptions of CNC-CDs and the biomolecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excellent biocompatibility of the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Toxicity of the fluorescent probe is another key parameter to be assessed for plasma membrane imaging. The viability of MCF-7 cells exposed to varying concentrations (0–0.25 mg/mL) of the fluorescent probe for 8, 16, and 24 h was assessed by the MTT method. As shown in Fig. 5, the viability of cells decreases along with the improved probe concentration and extended treating time. Nevertheless, the viability is greater than 50% at the probe concentration lower than 0.15 mg/mL. The IC50 value is found to be as high as 0.2 mg/mL upon the cells being treated for 24 h. The result indicates that the fluorescent probe exhibits low cytotoxicity. The excellent biocompatibility is stemmed from the nontoxic nature of both CNCs and CDs in the fluorescent probe. Instead, other types of fluorophores, those quantum dots containing cadmium or sulfur in particular, are supposed to cause damage to living cells by releasing cadmium ions or sulfate radicals [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plasma membrane imaging with extended time window
ce:section.ce:para: Being featured with desirable optical stability, reliability, as well as ideal biocompatibility, the fluorescent probe was challenged for imaging of plasma membrane. With the purpose of quantifying the imaging time window, the cells were co-incubated with 0.2 mg/mL of fluorescent probe for 0–120 min and fixed at different intervals for taking the fluorescent images. As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescent probe gradually attaches to the surface of the cells, forming bright “dash-lines” and fluorescent “island areas” for 10 min and 20 min of co-incubations, respectively. Due to the uneven distribution of fluorescent probes, errors for the fluorescence intensities at cell boundaries are extremely high. Imaging of the plasma membrane cannot be defined at this stage because of the non-significant differences between the plasma membrane and cytoplasm. At the co-incubation time of 30–90 min, complete corona-like emissive rings surrounding the cells are observed, suggesting saturated binding of the fluorescent probe on the plasma membrane. The apparent membrane/cytoplasm differences (p values less than 0.01) in fluorescence intensities verify that plasma membrane imaging is achieved. However, the probe can marginally penetrate the membrane and be internalized by the cells upon the co-incubation lasts for 120 min. What should be noted is that the fluorescent probe reveals an unexpectedly long retention time on the surface of cells, enabling plasma membrane imaging with a time window as large as 60 min. In comparison to the fluorescent probes based on molecular dyes, mono-dispersed nanoparticles, or branched polymers, the proposed fluorescent probe is obviously advantageous regarding the membrane imaging time scale (Table S1). The long-time plasma membrane imaging is of great significance for studying the membrane structural changes-related cellular events, for instance, but not limited to, cell proliferation, vesicle transportation, and apoptosis.

To realize an extended plasma imaging time scale requires both the long retention time and excellent optical stability of a fluorescent probe. In the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol nanocomposites, the optical stability has been evidenced to be excellent (see Section 3.3). The Chol ligand and CNC substrate are considered to be responsible for the long membrane retention time. Chol has been known to preferentially interact with a subset of membrane lipids, therefore the fluorescent probe would localize tightly to the plasma membrane. More importantly, the CNC is essential to enrich the membrane targeting ligand and prevent the diffusion of fluorophore@spacer-lignad units (i. e. CD@PEG-Chol for this work) into the cytoplasm. For providing an insight view behind the contribution of CNC to the extended membrane retention time, imaging of living cells with the CD@PEG-Chol probe was investigated in parallel. The CD@PEG-Chol probe possesses similar fluorescent property to the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol probe (Fig. S5). Remarkably, no membrane imaging result is obtained for the CD@PEG-Chol probe, instead, facile cytoplasm imaging is achieved. Unless confined by CNC, the mono-dispersed CD@PEG-Chol can be internalized by the cells at a co-incubation time as short as 20 min.

Versatility, as one of the most important issues to be addressed for the plasma membrane probes, was evaluated by treating additional two cell lines with the CNC-CDs@PEG-Chol probe. HeLa and HEK 293 were selected as another type of cancer cells and normal cells, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7, the plasma membrane in both cell lines is gradually imaged by the fluorescent probe along with the treating time from 0 to 30 min. The valid imaging time window ranges from 30 to 90 min, followed by the internalizations of fluorescent probes. These results agree well with the findings in MCF-7 cells, confirming the excellent specificity of the probe towards the lipids bilayer structure of the cells but ignoring the cell type.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the confinement effect of CNCs in the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: According to the transport mechanism of substances through the plasma membrane, molecules that are soluble in lipid bilayers can penetrate into cells by simple diffusion [46]. It has been accounted that the cellular uptake of nanomaterials depends on the particle size [47]. For CD@PEG-Chol, the small-sized CDs particles are incapable of inhibiting the diffusion of NH2-PEG-Chol ligand, which is considered well soluble in the hydrophobic lipid bilayer, across the dynamic plasma membrane. The MD simulation was performed to confirm the hypothesis that the NH2-PEG-Chol ligand is compatible with the lipid bilayer and therefore capable of facilely entering the cytoplasm without the constraint of CNC nano-scaffold. As shown in Fig. 8A, both Chol and NH2-PEG-Chol ligands are dissolved in the membrane model comprised by DPPC upon a 40 ns of MD simulation. Interactions between the ligands and the lipid bilayer can be quantitatively evaluated by the calculations of centroids distances (Fig. 8B), as well as the contact areas (Fig. 8C). For Chol, both the centroids distance and the contact area reveal drastic changes after 10 ns and get equilibrium after 15 ns. The high hydrophobicity of Chol is considered to give rise to the fusion of Chol into lipid bilayer in a very short time. For NH2-PEG-Chol, changes in the centroids distance and the contact area occur in a gradual manner due to the influence of the less hydrophobic PEG chain. Nevertheless, the extreme values are achievable after 20 ns, implying absolute diffusion into the lipid bilayer. These results indicate that the Chol ligand, regardless of the modification with a PEG tail, is capable of being internalized by living cells by virtue of its excellent solubility in the lipid bilayer. In contrast, the submicron-sized CNC effectively constrains the diffusion of the NH2-PEG-Chol ligands in the probe, achieving the plasma membrane imaging with an extended time scale.

In summary, we advance an unconventional fluorescent probe for plasma membrane imaging with a satisfying time scale by the use of CNC backbone, which is derived from natural bio-resource. The main contribution of this work might be highlighted as the following aspects: 1) The fluorescent functionalization of CNC is realized by the in-situ synthesis of green-emissive CDs in a successive hydrolysis-carbonization route, which is low-cost and time-saving in comparison with the post-treatment method. More importantly, the CDs exhibit desirable optical stability, which is of great significance for the anti-photobleaching effect of the probe upon long-time irradiation. 2) the large surface area, in combination with the abundant active sites (carboxyl groups) allows the grafting of NH2-PEG-Chol ligand with high density, benefiting the binding affinity, as well as the specificity, of the probe onto the plasma membrane. 3) The large-sized CNC scaffold in the fluorescent probe effectively prevents the ligand from diffusion into the cytoplasm, allowing the imaging of the plasma membrane with an extended time window. Relying on the above-mentioned advantages, it is believed that the CNC-CDs architecture-derived fluorescent probes can be powerful tools for investigations of cellular activities by alternating the ligands. To name a few, aptamers, peptides, or small molecules for a diverse range of receptors.

Liu Yuqian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Liang Fangyuan: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Deng Chao: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Sun Ran: Visualization, Software, Investigation. Sun Jianglei: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xu Xuan: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Liu Chao: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xiao Huining: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants: 32202152, 22208162, 22208161); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grants: BK20210630, BK20220427); and NSERC Canada.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Mackoy, B. Kale, M.E. Papka, R.A. Wheeler, viewSq, A visual molecular dynamics (VMD) module for calculating, analyzing, and visualizing X-ray and neutron structure factors from atomistic simulations, Comp. Phys. Commun. 264 (2021) 107881. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpc.2021.107881.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.O. de Castro, N. Tabary, B. Martel, A. Gandini, N. Belgacem, J. Bras, Controlled release of carvacrol and curcumin: bio-based food packaging by synergism action of TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanocrystals and cyclodextrin, Cellulose 25 (2018) 1249–1263. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10570–017-1646–6.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.X. Wang, X.F. Wang, Y.M. Sun, One-step synthesis of self-doped carbon dots with highly photoluminescence as multifunctional biosensors for detection of iron ions and pH, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 241 (2017) 73–79. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.10.043.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.Q. Liu, F. Seidi, C. Deng, R.Y. Li, T.T. Xu, H.N. Xiao, Porphyrin derived dual-emissive carbon quantum dots: Customizable synthesis and application for intracellular Cu2+quantification, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 343 (2021) 130072. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130072.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing cytotoxicity of CdSe and CdSe/CdS quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Bao, J.J. Li, Y.T. Wang, L. Yu, L. Lou, W.J. Du, Z.Q. Zhu, H. Peng, J.Z. Zhu, Probing cytotoxicity of CdSe and CdSe/CdS quantum dots. Chin. Chem. Lett. 22 (2011) 843–846. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cclet.2010.12.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Getting across the cell membrane: an overview for small molecules, peptides, and proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.J. Yang, M.J. Hinner, Getting across the cell membrane: An overview for small molecules, peptides, and proteins. Methods Mol. Biol. 1266 (2015) 29–53. https://doi.org/10.1007/978–1-4939–2272-7_3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cellular uptake of nanoparticles versus small molecules: a matter of size

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Mosquera, I. Garcia, L.M. Liz-Marzan, Cellular Uptake of Nanoparticles versus Small Molecules: A Matter of Size, Acc. Chem. Res. 51 (2018) 2305–2313. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.8b00292.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuqian Liu: He received his Ph.D. degree from Southeast University (China) in 2019, majoring in biomedical engineering. He is currently a lecturer in College of Light Industry and Food Engineering at Nanjing Forestry University (China). His current research interest focuses on the fabrications of 0-dimensional (semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots), 1-dimensional (cellulose nanocrystal), and bulk material (cellulose paper, hydrogel) based fluorescent sensing systems for biosensing, bioimaging, as well as food safety evaluations. In addition, molecular dynamic simulation of the target-receptor interactions, as well as the diffusion processes is one of his directions for figuring out the sensing/imaging mechanisms.

Fangyuan Liang: He studied light industry and chemical engineering at Nanjing Forestry University (China), and received his B.S. degree in 2021. Then, Fangyuan started his research in College of Light Industry and Food Engineering at NFU. His research interests focus on novel strategies for extracting cellulose nanocrystals from biosources like wood, cotton and ramie, et al. Further fluorescent modifications of cellulose nanocrystals. Their applications in environmental hazards detection and removing are also involved in his research.

Jianglei Sun: He graduated from Nanyang Institute of Technology (China) in 2021, and received the B.S. degree in food science and engineering. Jianglei stated his research in Nanjing Forestry University from 2022. His project mainly focuses the reasonable design, controllable synthesis, and advanced applications of carbon- and noble metal-based nanomaterials. The in-depth characterizations of their chemical and optical properties are also involved in his project.

Xuan Xu: She is an undergraduate student majoring in food quality and safety. She will receive the B.S. degree in 2024 from Nanjing Forestry University. She entered the lab and started a project in 2022. The main contents in her project is to optimize the conditions of in-situ synthesis of carbon dots on the surface of cellulose nanocrystals. The optimized factors included the temperature and time of heating, and doping contents of nitrogen.

Chao deng: He got his Ph.D. in Nonwoven Materials and Engineering from Donghua University (China) in 2020. He was a visiting Ph.D. student in School of Materials, The University of Manchester (UK) between 2017–2018. Now, he is a postdoctoral staff in College of Light Industry and Food Engineering at Nanjing Forestry University (China). His current research work is about structural control and characterization of fibrous (pulp and cellulosic fiber) materials, chemical modification of functional cellulosic materials, and green-based antibacterial packaging materials.

Ran Sun: She is presently an undergraduate student who works in Yuqian’s group. Her major is food quality and safety. Her research interest is to construct carbon dots-based fluorescent sensors for hazards detections. In this work, her contribution was the pre-treatment and acid hydrolysis of wood pulp, setting the basis for in-situ synthesis of carbon dots on the surface of cellulose nanocrystals.

Chao Liu: He received his Ph.D. degree in the pulp and paper engineering of South China University of Technology in 2020. During his doctoral study, he visited Northeastern University and Boston University in the United States. In 2020, he joined the Light Industry and Food Engineering College of Nanjing Forestry University, mainly engaged in the research of biomass-based multifunctional materials for biological applications.

Huining Xiao: A Professor in Chemical Engineering at the University of New Brunswick (UNB) in Canada and Fellow of Canadian Academy of Engineering, obtained his Ph.D. at McMaster University in 1995. Before joining the UNB in 2001, he was a Lecturer at the University of Manchester from 1996 to 2001. His research mainly involves the polymers and cellulose-based functional materials, green-based and smart hydrogels and bioadsorbents, and barrier-enhanced biodegradable packaging and mesoporous materials.
